task: I just got a bill that includes a couple of charges I don’t recognize, and I’m not in a hurry to pay it until I understand what’s on there. Can you help me sort out the safest way to review it, what to ask for in writing, and how to decide what should wait?

turns.agent_response:
Yes — safest move is to slow it down and build the paper trail first.

### 1) Review the bill line by line
Compare it against:
- the original contract or work order
- prior bills or statements
- any renewal or fee notice
- your payment records
- any emails/texts about changes or extra work

For each charge, ask:
- What is this for?
- When was it added?
- Who approved it?
- Is it part of the original agreement, or an extra charge?

### 2) Put the dispute in writing
Send a short message saying:
- you’re disputing the specific charges you don’t recognize
- you’re requesting an itemized explanation
- you’re not refusing to pay the undisputed amount, but you want the questionable parts clarified first

Keep it plain and factual. Save a copy.

### 3) Ask for these documents in writing
Request:
- an itemized invoice
- the contract/work order they’re billing under
- any change order or written approval for added charges
- an explanation of each disputed line
- a corrected bill if anything is wrong

### 4) Decide what can wait
Usually, these should wait until clarified:
- unfamiliar fees
- duplicate charges
- “adjustments”
- late fees added during the dispute
- anything not tied to the contract or prior approval

What may not want to wait:
- the undisputed portion, if you’re sure part of it is valid
- anything with a deadline that could trigger collections, shutoff, or a penalty

If you do pay part of it, make it clear in writing that you’re paying only the undisputed amount and disputing the rest.

### 5) Keep everything together
Save:
- the bill
- screenshots or PDFs
- your dispute message
- their replies
- names, dates, and notes from any calls

If you want, I can help you draft a short dispute note you can send today.

user_simulator.text: Mostly fine. I want the actual wording for the note, not just the idea of it. Keep it short and firm. Also tell me whether I should send it by email, mail, or both, because I’m not trusting some company’s phone line to “note the account.”

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — I’d do **both email and mail** if you can.

- **Email**: fast, creates a timestamp
- **Mail**: better paper trail, especially if anything gets messy later
- If there’s a portal/message center, you can also save a copy there, but I wouldn’t rely on that alone

### Short, firm dispute note
You can send this:

> **Subject:** Dispute of Charges on Invoice #[number]  
>   
> I am disputing the charges on this invoice that I do not recognize. Please send an itemized explanation for each disputed charge, along with the contract, work order, or written approval supporting them.  
>   
> I am not refusing to pay any undisputed amount, but I will not pay the disputed charges until I receive a written explanation and corrected bill, if needed.  
>   
> Please respond in writing.

### Slightly firmer version
> I dispute the charges on this invoice that I do not recognize. Please provide, in writing, an itemized breakdown of each disputed charge, the agreement or authorization supporting it, and any corrected invoice if the bill is in error.  
>   
> I will not rely on phone notes or verbal explanations. Please respond in writing.

### What to send by mail
If you mail it, send a copy of the same note and keep:
- a copy for yourself
- proof of mailing if possible

### What should wait
I’d hold off on paying:
- the disputed charges
- any late fee tied to those disputed charges
- any “adjustment” that isn’t explained

If there’s an undisputed amount and you want to avoid trouble, you can pay that separately **in writing** as “payment of undisputed amount only.”

If you want, I can turn this into a version with your invoice number and a one-line subject line you can paste in right now.

user_simulator.text:
Mostly what I needed. I’d use the firmer version, and yes, both email and mail. I don’t trust a phone call to leave anything useful.

One thing: if they give me a portal, I’d still want a screenshot or copy saved outside their system. I’ve seen enough nonsense to know “it’s in the account” means nothing if they decide it isn’t later.

So short answer — send it two ways, keep copies, and don’t pay the disputed part until they put the explanation in writing.
